expanded_description: A comprehensive exploration of how electronegativity differences between atoms determine bond polarity, creating a continuum from nonpolar covalent to ionic bonds, and how this affects molecular properties and chemical behavior.

core_concept.principle: Bond polarity is determined by the electronegativity difference (ΔEN) between bonded atoms, creating a continuum from nonpolar covalent (ΔEN < 0.4) to polar covalent (0.4 ≤ ΔEN < 1.7) to ionic (ΔEN ≥ 1.7) bonds.
core_concept.explanation: Electronegativity measures an atom's ability to attract shared electrons in a chemical bond. When two atoms with different electronegativities bond, the more electronegative atom pulls the shared electrons closer, creating an unequal distribution of charge. This creates a polar bond with partial positive (δ⁺) and partial negative (δ⁻) charges. The greater the electronegativity difference, the more polar the bond. Understanding bond polarity is essential for predicting molecular behavior, solubility, reactivity, and intermolecular forces.

image.alt: Diagram showing bond polarity continuum from nonpolar covalent to ionic based on electronegativity difference
image.caption: Bond polarity continuum: electronegativity difference determines bond type and polarity.

sections.introduction: Electronegativity is one of the most important concepts in chemistry for understanding how atoms interact in bonds. When atoms with different electronegativities form bonds, the resulting electron distribution creates polarity that affects all aspects of molecular behavior.

key_concept.definition: Bond polarity refers to the unequal distribution of electrons in a covalent bond due to differences in electronegativity between the bonded atoms, creating partial charges (δ⁺ and δ⁻) on the atoms.
key_concept.context: Bond polarity exists on a continuum: nonpolar covalent bonds have equal electron sharing, polar covalent bonds have unequal sharing, and ionic bonds have complete electron transfer. The electronegativity difference determines where a bond falls on this continuum.

principles.statement: The difference in electronegativity (ΔEN) between two bonded atoms predicts bond type: ΔEN < 0.4 = nonpolar covalent, 0.4 ≤ ΔEN < 1.7 = polar covalent, ΔEN ≥ 1.7 = ionic.
principles.application: Enables prediction of bond type and properties from periodic table position; explains why some compounds are soluble in water while others are not.
principles.example: H-H: ΔEN = 0 (nonpolar); H-Cl: ΔEN = 0.96 (polar covalent); Na-Cl: ΔEN = 2.23 (ionic).
principles.analogy: Like a tug-of-war: equal strength teams (same EN) = equal sharing (nonpolar); unequal teams (different EN) = unequal sharing (polar); very unequal = one team wins completely (ionic).

image.alt: Chart showing electronegativity difference ranges for different bond types
image.caption: Electronegativity difference chart: guides bond type prediction based on ΔEN values.

principles.statement: In polar covalent bonds, the more electronegative atom acquires a partial negative charge (δ⁻) and the less electronegative atom acquires a partial positive charge (δ⁺).
principles.application: Explains molecular dipole moments, intermolecular forces, solubility patterns, and chemical reactivity of polar molecules.
principles.example: H-Cl bond: Cl (EN=3.16) is δ⁻, H (EN=2.20) is δ⁺. The bond has a dipole moment pointing from H to Cl.
principles.analogy: Like a magnet: one end is positive (δ⁺), one end is negative (δ⁻), creating a directional pull.

image.alt: Diagram showing δ⁺ and δ⁻ partial charges in polar bonds
image.caption: Partial charges: more electronegative atom is δ⁻, less electronegative is δ⁺.

principles.statement: A polar bond has a dipole moment (μ) that measures the magnitude of charge separation; μ = Q × r, where Q is charge magnitude and r is distance between charges.
principles.application: Quantifies bond polarity; larger dipole moments indicate more polar bonds; affects molecular interactions and physical properties.
principles.example: H-F has larger dipole moment (1.82 D) than H-Cl (1.08 D) because F is more electronegative than Cl, creating greater charge separation.
principles.analogy: Like a battery: stronger battery (larger dipole) has greater potential difference between terminals (charges).

image.alt: Diagram showing bond dipole moment with arrow pointing to negative end
image.caption: Bond dipole moment: arrow points from δ⁺ to δ⁻, length indicates polarity magnitude.

components.definition: Electronegativity difference and bond polarity relationship
components.interpretation: ΔEN < 0.4: nonpolar covalent (equal sharing); 0.4 ≤ ΔEN < 1.7: polar covalent (unequal sharing); ΔEN ≥ 1.7: ionic (electron transfer). Larger ΔEN = more polar bond = larger dipole moment.

worked_examples.title: Calculating Electronegativity Difference and Predicting Bond Type
worked_examples.problem: Calculate the electronegativity difference and predict the bond type for: (a) C-H, (b) N-O, (c) K-F. Use EN values: C=2.55, H=2.20, N=3.04, O=3.44, K=0.82, F=3.98.

step_by_step.explanation: Classify C-H bond based on ΔEN.
step_by_step.calculation: 0.35 < 0.4 → nonpolar covalent bond

step_by_step.explanation: Classify N-O bond based on ΔEN.
step_by_step.calculation: 0.40 ≤ ΔEN < 1.7 → polar covalent bond (O is δ⁻, N is δ⁺)

step_by_step.explanation: Classify K-F bond based on ΔEN.
step_by_step.calculation: 3.16 ≥ 1.7 → ionic bond (K⁺ and F⁻)

worked_examples.final_answer: (a) C-H: ΔEN = 0.35 → nonpolar covalent. (b) N-O: ΔEN = 0.40 → polar covalent (O is δ⁻, N is δ⁺). (c) K-F: ΔEN = 3.16 → ionic (K⁺F⁻). Electronegativity difference reliably predicts bond type and polarity.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Using electronegativity difference to predict bond type and polarity

worked_examples.title: Determining Partial Charges in Polar Bonds
worked_examples.problem: For each polar bond, identify which atom is δ⁺ and which is δ⁻: (a) C-O, (b) H-F, (c) S-Cl. Use EN values: C=2.55, O=3.44, H=2.20, F=3.98, S=2.58, Cl=3.16.

step_by_step.explanation: Compare EN values for C-O bond.
step_by_step.calculation: C=2.55, O=3.44; O is more electronegative
C is δ⁺ (less EN), O is δ⁻ (more EN)

step_by_step.explanation: Compare EN values for H-F bond.
step_by_step.calculation: H=2.20, F=3.98; F is much more electronegative
H is δ⁺ (less EN), F is δ⁻ (more EN); highly polar bond

step_by_step.explanation: Compare EN values for S-Cl bond.
step_by_step.calculation: S=2.58, Cl=3.16; Cl is more electronegative
S is δ⁺ (less EN), Cl is δ⁻ (more EN)

worked_examples.final_answer: (a) C-O: C is δ⁺, O is δ⁻. (b) H-F: H is δ⁺, F is δ⁻ (most polar). (c) S-Cl: S is δ⁺, Cl is δ⁻. The more electronegative atom always acquires δ⁻ charge in polar bonds.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Assigning partial charges based on relative electronegativity values

sections.introduction: Bond polarity is not a simple either/or classification but exists on a continuum from completely nonpolar to completely ionic. Understanding this continuum helps explain why some bonds have mixed character and why properties change gradually across compound series.

key_concept.definition: The bond polarity continuum ranges from nonpolar covalent (equal electron sharing, ΔEN ≈ 0) through polar covalent (unequal sharing, 0.4 ≤ ΔEN < 1.7) to ionic (electron transfer, ΔEN ≥ 1.7), with no sharp boundaries between categories.
key_concept.context: The cutoff values (0.4 and 1.7) are guidelines, not absolute rules; some bonds near the boundaries have mixed character. Percent ionic character can be calculated for any bond.

classifications.value: ΔEN < 0.4; electrons shared equally or nearly equally
characteristics: Equal or nearly equal electron distribution
Typically between identical atoms or similar EN
classifications.behavior: Electrons spend equal time near both nuclei; no permanent dipole; molecules are typically nonpolar if symmetric.

examples.explanation: ΔEN = |2.20 - 2.20| = 0. Perfectly nonpolar; electrons shared equally between identical H atoms. No partial charges.

image.alt: H₂ molecule showing equal electron sharing
image.caption: Nonpolar bond: identical atoms share electrons equally.

examples.explanation: ΔEN = |2.55 - 2.20| = 0.35 < 0.4. Considered nonpolar despite slight EN difference; electrons nearly equally shared. CH₄ is nonpolar molecule.

image.alt: Methane showing C-H bonds
image.caption: C-H bonds: slightly polar but considered nonpolar (ΔEN < 0.4).

examples.explanation: ΔEN = |3.16 - 3.16| = 0. Perfectly nonpolar; identical atoms. Diatomic halogens all have nonpolar bonds.

image.alt: Cl₂ molecule showing equal electron sharing
image.caption: Cl-Cl bond: identical atoms, perfectly nonpolar.

classifications.behavior: Electrons spend more time near more electronegative atom; creates bond dipole; molecules may be polar or nonpolar depending on geometry.

examples.explanation: ΔEN = |2.20 - 3.16| = 0.96. Cl is δ⁻, H is δ⁺. Electrons spend more time near Cl. Creates molecular dipole. HCl is polar molecule.

image.alt: HCl molecule showing polar bond with partial charges
image.caption: Polar covalent bond: unequal electron sharing creates partial charges.

examples.explanation: ΔEN = |2.20 - 3.44| = 1.24. O is δ⁻, H is δ⁺. Highly polar bonds. Combined with bent geometry, creates strong molecular dipole and hydrogen bonding.

image.alt: Water molecule showing polar O-H bonds
image.caption: O-H bonds: highly polar (ΔEN = 1.24), enables hydrogen bonding.

examples.explanation: ΔEN = |2.55 - 3.44| = 0.89. O is δ⁻, C is δ⁺. Individual bonds are polar, but linear geometry causes bond dipoles to cancel, making CO₂ nonpolar molecule.

image.alt: CO₂ molecule showing polar bonds but nonpolar molecule
image.caption: CO₂: polar bonds but nonpolar molecule due to symmetric geometry.

classifications.value: ΔEN ≥ 1.7; electrons transferred rather than shared
classifications.behavior: Electrons effectively transferred from metal to nonmetal; forms ions held by electrostatic forces; conducts when molten or dissolved.

examples.explanation: ΔEN = |0.93 - 3.16| = 2.23 ≥ 1.7. Na transfers electron to Cl, forming Na⁺ and Cl⁻. Ionic lattice, not discrete molecules. High mp (801°C).

image.alt: NaCl crystal lattice showing ionic bonding
image.caption: Ionic bond: electron transfer creates ions held by electrostatic attraction.

examples.explanation: ΔEN = |1.31 - 3.44| = 2.13 ≥ 1.7. Mg transfers 2 electrons to O, forming Mg²⁺ and O²⁻. Very high mp (2852°C) due to +2/-2 charges.

image.alt: MgO crystal lattice showing ionic bonding
image.caption: MgO: +2/-2 charges create very strong ionic bonding.

examples.explanation: ΔEN = |0.82 - 3.98| = 3.16. One of the most ionic bonds possible. K transfers electron to F, forming K⁺ and F⁻. Very high lattice energy.

image.alt: KF crystal lattice showing ionic bonding
image.caption: K-F: very large ΔEN (3.16), highly ionic bond.

classifications.value: Quantitative measure of bond polarity on continuum
classifications.behavior: No bond is 100% ionic; even NaCl has ~70% ionic character. Percent ionic character increases with ΔEN but never reaches 100%.

examples.explanation: Formula: % Ionic = (1 - e^(-0.25(ΔEN)²)) × 100%. H-Cl (ΔEN=0.96): ~20% ionic. Na-Cl (ΔEN=2.23): ~70% ionic. No bond is 100% ionic.

image.alt: Graph showing percent ionic character vs. electronegativity difference
image.caption: Percent ionic character: increases with ΔEN but never reaches 100%.

examples.explanation: H-F: ΔEN = 1.78, ~55% ionic character. Borderline polar covalent/ionic. Exists as molecules (covalent) but highly polar.

image.alt: HF molecule showing high polarity
image.caption: H-F bond: 55% ionic character, highly polar covalent.

principles.principle: No Sharp Boundaries Between Bond Types
principles.statement: The cutoff values (0.4 and 1.7) are guidelines; bonds near these values have mixed character and properties intermediate between categories.
principles.application: Explains why some 'ionic' compounds have covalent character (AlCl₃); why some 'covalent' compounds have ionic character (HF).
principles.example: Al-Cl: ΔEN = 1.55 (polar covalent by guidelines), but AlCl₃ behaves ionically in some contexts. Bond type depends on conditions.
principles.analogy: Like color spectrum: no sharp line between blue and green; there's a transition region with mixed colors.

image.alt: Continuum diagram showing gradual transition between bond types
image.caption: Bond type continuum: no sharp boundaries, gradual transition between categories.

principles.principle: Bond Properties Change Gradually with ΔEN
principles.statement: Physical and chemical properties change gradually as ΔEN increases; no sudden property changes at the guideline boundaries.
principles.application: Explains why melting points, solubility, and conductivity change gradually across compound series, not suddenly.
principles.example: Melting points: CH₄ (-182°C) < CH₃Cl (-97°C) < CH₂Cl₂ (-97°C) < CHCl₃ (-63°C) < CCl₄ (-23°C). Gradual change with increasing polarity.
principles.analogy: Like temperature: no sudden change at 0°C for most properties; water gradually gets colder, then suddenly freezes at specific point.

worked_examples.title: Calculating Percent Ionic Character
worked_examples.problem: Calculate the percent ionic character for: (a) H-Cl (ΔEN = 0.96), (b) Na-Cl (ΔEN = 2.23), (c) C-O (ΔEN = 0.89). Use formula: % Ionic = (1 - e^(-0.25(ΔEN)²)) × 100%.

step_by_step.calculation: H-Cl: 21% ionic (polar covalent); Na-Cl: 71% ionic (mostly ionic); C-O: 18% ionic (polar covalent)

worked_examples.final_answer: (a) H-Cl: 21% ionic character (polar covalent). (b) Na-Cl: 71% ionic character (mostly ionic, but still has covalent character). (c) C-O: 18% ionic character (polar covalent). Even 'ionic' bonds like Na-Cl are not 100% ionic; all bonds have some covalent character.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Calculating percent ionic character to quantify bond polarity on the continuum

worked_examples.title: Ranking Bonds by Polarity
worked_examples.problem: Rank the following bonds in order of increasing polarity: C-H, O-H, N-H, F-H. Use EN values: C=2.55, H=2.20, O=3.44, N=3.04, F=3.98.

step_by_step.explanation: Rank by ΔEN (larger ΔEN = more polar).

step_by_step.calculation: C-H: nonpolar covalent; N-H: polar covalent; O-H: polar covalent; F-H: polar covalent/ionic borderline

step_by_step.explanation: Identify δ⁺ and δ⁻ for each bond.
step_by_step.calculation: All have H as δ⁺ (least EN); C/N/O/F are δ⁻ (more EN)

worked_examples.final_answer: Increasing polarity: C-H < N-H < O-H < F-H. C-H is essentially nonpolar (ΔEN=0.35); N-H, O-H, and F-H are progressively more polar. F-H is most polar (ΔEN=1.78), approaching ionic character. All have H as δ⁺ and the other atom as δ⁻.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Ranking bonds by polarity using electronegativity differences

sections.introduction: Electronegativity is not a fixed property but varies systematically across the periodic table based on atomic structure. Understanding these trends enables prediction of electronegativity values and bond polarities for any element combination.

key_concept.definition: Electronegativity is determined by effective nuclear charge (Z_eff) and atomic radius: higher Z_eff and smaller radius increase electronegativity by strengthening the nucleus's pull on bonding electrons.
key_concept.context: Electronegativity increases across periods (increasing Z_eff) and decreases down groups (increasing radius); F is most electronegative (3.98), Fr is least (~0.7).

principles.statement: Higher effective nuclear charge (Z_eff) increases electronegativity because the nucleus attracts bonding electrons more strongly.
principles.application: Explains why electronegativity increases across periods: more protons with similar shielding = higher Z_eff = higher EN.
principles.example: Period 2: Li (Z_eff≈+1, EN=0.98) < C (Z_eff≈+4, EN=2.55) < F (Z_eff≈+7, EN=3.98). Increasing Z_eff increases EN.
principles.analogy: Like a stronger magnet: more protons (stronger magnet) pull electrons (metal filings) more strongly.

image.alt: Graph showing relationship between effective nuclear charge and electronegativity
image.caption: Effective nuclear charge: higher Z_eff increases electronegativity across periods.

principles.statement: Smaller atomic radius increases electronegativity because bonding electrons are closer to the nucleus and experience stronger attraction.
principles.application: Explains why electronegativity decreases down groups: larger radius = bonding electrons farther from nucleus = lower EN.
principles.example: Group 17: F (71 pm, EN=3.98) > Cl (99 pm, EN=3.16) > Br (114 pm, EN=2.96) > I (133 pm, EN=2.66). Larger radius = lower EN.
principles.analogy: Like a shorter rope: shorter distance (smaller radius) = stronger pull on the object (bonding electrons).

image.alt: Graph showing inverse relationship between atomic radius and electronegativity
image.caption: Atomic radius: smaller atoms have higher electronegativity due to closer bonding electrons.

principles.statement: Electronegativity increases from bottom-left to top-right of periodic table; F is most electronegative, Fr is least electronegative.
principles.application: Enables prediction of relative EN for any elements; explains why certain bonds are more polar than others.
principles.example: Most polar bonds: F with metals (Na-F, K-F). Least polar: metals with metals (Na-K) or identical nonmetals (Cl-Cl).

image.alt: Periodic table colored by electronegativity showing trend from low to high
image.caption: Electronegativity trend: increases from bottom-left (Fr, 0.7) to top-right (F, 3.98).

principles.statement: Atom electronegativity increases with s-character in hybridization: sp (50% s) > sp² (33% s) > sp³ (25% s).
principles.application: Explains why alkynes (sp C) are more acidic than alkenes (sp² C) or alkanes (sp³ C); affects bond polarity in organic molecules.
principles.example: C-H bond polarity: sp C-H (alkyne) > sp² C-H (alkene) > sp³ C-H (alkane). More s-character = C more electronegative = H more acidic.
principles.analogy: Like different grip strengths: tighter grip (more s-character) = stronger pull on electrons.

components.definition: Electronegativity depends on atomic structure factors
components.equation: $\text{EN} \propto \dfrac{Z_{eff}}{r^2}$ where Z_eff = effective nuclear charge, r = atomic radius
components.interpretation: Higher Z_eff and smaller r both increase EN. Across periods: Z_eff dominates (EN increases). Down groups: r dominates (EN decreases).

worked_examples.title: Predicting Electronegativity from Periodic Position
worked_examples.problem: Without looking up values, predict which element is more electronegative: (a) N or O, (b) Cl or Br, (c) C or Si. Explain using periodic trends.

step_by_step.calculation: N (Group 15) and O (Group 16) both Period 2; O is to the right → O has higher Z_eff → O more electronegative
Cl (Period 3) and Br (Period 4) both Group 17; Cl is above Br → Cl has smaller radius → Cl more electronegative
C (Period 2) and Si (Period 3) both Group 14; C is above Si → C has smaller radius → C more electronegative

worked_examples.final_answer: (a) O > N (O is to the right in same period, higher Z_eff). (b) Cl > Br (Cl is above Br in same group, smaller radius). (c) C > Si (C is above Si in same group, smaller radius). Periodic trends reliably predict relative electronegativity without memorizing values.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Using periodic trends to predict relative electronegativity values

worked_examples.title: Explaining Electronegativity Trend Exceptions
worked_examples.problem: Explain why Ga (EN=1.81) has similar electronegativity to Al (EN=1.61) despite being below Al in Group 13, when EN typically decreases down groups.

step_by_step.calculation: EN typically decreases down groups due to increasing radius; Ga should have lower EN than Al
Ga: [Ar]4s²3d¹⁰4p¹; Al: [Ne]3s²3p¹. Ga has filled 3d¹⁰ subshell between core and valence
3d electrons shield poorly; Ga experiences higher Z_eff than expected; radius smaller than expected

step_by_step.explanation: Conclude why Ga has higher EN than expected.
step_by_step.calculation: Poor d-electron shielding increases Z_eff for Ga, offsetting radius increase → Ga EN similar to or slightly higher than Al

worked_examples.final_answer: Ga has similar electronegativity to Al (Ga=1.81, Al=1.61) despite being below Al because Ga's filled 3d¹⁰ subshell shields poorly. This increases Ga's effective nuclear charge, offsetting the expected radius increase. This is an example of d-block contraction affecting periodic trends. Similar effects occur with f-block (lanthanide contraction).
worked_examples.concept_applied: Understanding exceptions to electronegativity trends due to d-block contraction

sections.introduction: Bond polarity directly affects molecular properties including solubility, boiling point, reactivity, and intermolecular forces. Understanding these relationships enables prediction of compound behavior from structure.

key_concept.definition: Bond polarity affects molecular properties through dipole-dipole interactions, hydrogen bonding, and solubility patterns; polar molecules interact differently than nonpolar molecules with other substances.
key_concept.context: Molecular polarity (not just bond polarity) determines many properties; molecular geometry can cancel bond dipoles, making molecules nonpolar despite polar bonds.

principles.statement: Polar molecules dissolve in polar solvents; nonpolar molecules dissolve in nonpolar solvents. Bond polarity determines molecular polarity and solubility.
principles.application: Predicts solubility: NaCl (ionic) dissolves in H₂O (polar) but not hexane (nonpolar); oil (nonpolar) dissolves in hexane but not water.
principles.example: Ethanol (C₂H₅OH, polar) mixes with water (polar) in all proportions. Hexane (C₆H₁₄, nonpolar) doesn't mix with water but mixes with oil.
principles.analogy: Like social groups: similar people (polar/polar or nonpolar/nonpolar) mix well; different people (polar/nonpolar) don't mix.

image.alt: Diagram showing polar and nonpolar substances mixing or separating
image.caption: Solubility: polar dissolves in polar, nonpolar dissolves in nonpolar ('like dissolves like').

principles.statement: More polar molecules have stronger intermolecular forces (dipole-dipole, hydrogen bonding), resulting in higher boiling points than nonpolar molecules of similar size.
principles.application: Explains why H₂O (bp 100°C) has much higher bp than CH₄ (bp -161°C) despite similar molar mass; predicts relative boiling points.
principles.example: H₂O (polar, H-bonding): bp 100°C. H₂S (less polar, no H-bonding): bp -60°C. Same group, similar mass, very different bp due to polarity.

image.alt: Graph showing boiling point vs. molecular polarity for similar-sized molecules
image.caption: Boiling point: more polar molecules have higher boiling points due to stronger intermolecular forces.

principles.statement: Polar bonds are more reactive than nonpolar bonds because partial charges create sites for nucleophilic and electrophilic attack.
principles.application: Explains why C=O bonds are reactive (C is δ⁺, attacked by nucleophiles); why C-H bonds are unreactive (nonpolar).
principles.example: Carbonyl compounds (C=O, polar) undergo nucleophilic addition; alkanes (C-H, nonpolar) are relatively inert except under extreme conditions.
principles.analogy: Like a magnet with exposed poles: polar bonds have exposed charges (poles) that attract other molecules; nonpolar bonds have no exposed charges.

principles.statement: Polar bonds absorb infrared radiation at characteristic frequencies; IR spectroscopy identifies functional groups based on bond polarity and vibration.
principles.application: IR spectroscopy detects polar bonds (C=O, O-H, N-H) but not nonpolar bonds (C-C, C-H weak); essential for structure determination.
principles.example: C=O stretch at 1700 cm⁻¹ (strong, polar); O-H stretch at 3300 cm⁻¹ (broad, polar); C-H stretch at 2900 cm⁻¹ (weak, less polar).

image.alt: IR spectrum showing peaks for polar bonds
image.caption: IR spectroscopy: polar bonds absorb at characteristic frequencies, enabling functional group identification.

worked_examples.title: Predicting Solubility from Bond Polarity
worked_examples.problem: Predict whether the following compounds will be soluble in water (polar) or hexane (nonpolar): (a) CH₃OH, (b) CCl₄, (c) NH₃. Explain using bond and molecular polarity.

step_by_step.calculation: C-O and O-H bonds are polar; molecule is polar (asymmetric); can H-bond with water → soluble in water
C-Cl bonds are polar, but molecule is symmetric (tetrahedral); bond dipoles cancel → nonpolar molecule → soluble in hexane, not water
N-H bonds are polar; molecule is polar (trigonal pyramidal); can H-bond with water → highly soluble in water
(a) CH₃OH: water; (b) CCl₄: hexane; (c) NH₃: water

worked_examples.final_answer: (a) CH₃OH: soluble in water (polar molecule, H-bonding). (b) CCl₄: soluble in hexane (nonpolar molecule despite polar bonds; symmetric geometry cancels dipoles). (c) NH₃: soluble in water (polar molecule, H-bonding). Molecular polarity (not just bond polarity) determines solubility.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Predicting solubility from bond and molecular polarity

worked_examples.title: Explaining Boiling Point Differences
worked_examples.problem: Explain why H₂O (bp 100°C) has a much higher boiling point than H₂S (bp -60°C) despite H₂S having higher molar mass (34 g/mol vs. 18 g/mol).

step_by_step.calculation: O-H: ΔEN = 1.24 (highly polar); S-H: ΔEN = 0.38 (weakly polar). O-H bonds much more polar.
H₂O: hydrogen bonding (strong); H₂S: dipole-dipole only (weak). H-bonding much stronger than dipole-dipole.
Both bent geometry, but O is more electronegative than S → H₂O has larger molecular dipole
H₂O H-bonding requires much more energy to overcome than H₂S dipole-dipole → H₂O bp 160°C higher despite lower mass

worked_examples.final_answer: H₂O has much higher boiling point (100°C vs. -60°C) because: (1) O-H bonds are more polar than S-H bonds (O more EN than S). (2) H₂O forms hydrogen bonds (strong intermolecular force); H₂S only has dipole-dipole forces (weak). (3) H-bonding requires much more energy to overcome. Bond polarity determines intermolecular force strength, which dominates over molar mass for boiling point.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Explaining physical property differences from bond polarity and intermolecular forces

sections.introduction: Understanding bond polarity has practical applications in drug design, materials science, environmental chemistry, and industrial processes. This knowledge enables rational design of molecules with desired properties.

key_concept.definition: Bond polarity knowledge enables prediction and design of molecular properties including solubility, reactivity, biological activity, and material characteristics for practical applications.
key_concept.context: From designing drugs that cross cell membranes to creating water-repellent materials, bond polarity understanding guides molecular design across chemistry disciplines.

classifications.value: Optimizing drug solubility and membrane permeability
classifications.behavior: Drugs need polar groups to dissolve in blood (aqueous) but nonpolar regions to cross cell membranes (lipid); bond polarity guides molecular design.

examples.explanation: Aspirin has polar carboxylic acid (-COOH) for water solubility and nonpolar benzene ring for membrane permeability. Balance enables oral absorption and blood transport.

image.alt: Aspirin structure showing polar and nonpolar regions
image.caption: Aspirin: polar -COOH group for solubility, nonpolar ring for membrane crossing.

examples.explanation: Replacing H with F (highly polar C-F bond) blocks metabolic sites while maintaining similar size; improves drug stability and half-life without losing activity.

image.alt: Drug structure showing fluorine substitution
image.caption: Fluorine substitution: polar C-F bond blocks metabolism, improves drug properties.

classifications.value: Creating molecules with both polar and nonpolar regions
classifications.behavior: Surfactants have polar heads that interact with water and nonpolar tails that interact with oil; enables mixing of oil and water (detergents, emulsifiers).

examples.explanation: Soap: polar carboxylate head (-COO⁻) + nonpolar hydrocarbon tail. Heads face water, tails face oil; forms micelles that trap oil for removal.

image.alt: Soap micelle structure showing polar heads and nonpolar tails
image.caption: Soap micelle: polar heads face water, nonpolar tails trap oil for removal.

examples.explanation: Cell membranes: phospholipids have polar phosphate heads and nonpolar fatty acid tails; form bilayer with heads facing water, tails facing inward.

image.alt: Cell membrane phospholipid bilayer structure
image.caption: Phospholipid bilayer: amphiphilic structure forms cell membrane barrier.

classifications.value: Choosing appropriate solvents for reactions and extractions
classifications.behavior: Solvent polarity affects reaction rates, product distribution, and purification; choosing correct solvent is critical for chemical processes.

examples.explanation: Separate polar from nonpolar compounds: polar compounds stay in water layer; nonpolar compounds move to organic layer (ether, hexane). Based on bond/molecular polarity.

image.alt: Liquid-liquid extraction showing polar and nonpolar layers
image.caption: Extraction: polar compounds in water layer, nonpolar in organic layer.

examples.explanation: Separate compounds by polarity: polar compounds stick to polar stationary phase; nonpolar compounds move faster with nonpolar mobile phase.

image.alt: Chromatography separation by polarity
image.caption: Chromatography: compounds separate based on polarity differences.

classifications.value: Designing materials with specific surface and bulk properties
classifications.behavior: Surface polarity determines wetting, adhesion, and biocompatibility; bond polarity guides material design for specific applications.

examples.explanation: Nonpolar coatings (fluoropolymers, silicones) repel water; used on fabrics, electronics, cookware. Low surface energy from nonpolar bonds prevents wetting.

image.alt: Water beading on hydrophobic surface
image.caption: Waterproof coating: nonpolar surface repels water, creates beading.

examples.explanation: Adhesives match polarity of surfaces being bonded: polar adhesives for polar surfaces (wood, paper); nonpolar adhesives for nonpolar surfaces (plastics).

image.alt: Adhesive bonding to different surfaces
image.caption: Adhesives: polarity matching ensures strong bonding to surfaces.

worked_examples.title: Designing a Molecule for Specific Solubility
worked_examples.problem: Design a molecule that will dissolve in both water and oil (amphiphilic). What structural features are needed? Give an example.

step_by_step.calculation: Need polar groups: -OH, -COOH, -NH₂, ionic groups (-COO⁻, -NH₃⁺) that can H-bond or ion-dipole interact with water
Need nonpolar regions: hydrocarbon chains (C-H, C-C bonds) that interact with oil via London dispersion forces

step_by_step.explanation: Combine both features in one molecule.
step_by_step.calculation: Amphiphilic structure: polar head group + nonpolar tail; ratio determines balance of water vs. oil solubility
Sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS): polar sulfate head (-OSO₃⁻Na⁺) + 12-carbon nonpolar tail; classic surfactant

worked_examples.final_answer: Amphiphilic molecule design: polar head group (e.g., -COO⁻, -OSO₃⁻, -OH) for water solubility + nonpolar hydrocarbon tail (C-H, C-C bonds) for oil solubility. Example: SDS (sodium dodecyl sulfate) has polar sulfate head and 12-carbon tail. This structure enables detergents to dissolve in water while trapping oil/grease in micelles.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Applying bond polarity knowledge to design amphiphilic molecules

worked_examples.title: Choosing Solvent for Chemical Reaction
worked_examples.problem: You need to run a reaction between an ionic compound (NaCN) and an organic compound (alkyl bromide). Should you use water, hexane, or a mixture? Explain using polarity principles.

step_by_step.calculation: NaCN: ionic (very polar), soluble in water, insoluble in hexane. Alkyl bromide: mostly nonpolar, soluble in hexane, low solubility in water
Both reactants must be in same phase to react; need solvent that dissolves both or phase transfer catalyst
Water alone: NaCN dissolves, alkyl bromide doesn't. Hexane alone: alkyl bromide dissolves, NaCN doesn't. Need polar aprotic solvent or mixture
Use polar aprotic solvent (DMSO, DMF, acetone) that dissolves both; or use phase transfer catalyst with water/organic mixture

worked_examples.final_answer: Neither water alone nor hexane alone will work. NaCN is ionic (water-soluble), alkyl bromide is nonpolar (hexane-soluble). Best options: (1) Polar aprotic solvent like DMSO or DMF that dissolves both reactants. (2) Two-phase system (water/hexane) with phase transfer catalyst to bring CN⁻ into organic phase. Bond polarity principles guide solvent selection for successful reactions.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Applying bond polarity to solvent selection for chemical reactions

summary: Bond polarity is determined by electronegativity difference (ΔEN) between bonded atoms: ΔEN < 0.4 = nonpolar covalent (equal sharing, e.g., H-H, C-H); 0.4 ≤ ΔEN < 1.7 = polar covalent (unequal sharing with δ⁺ and δ⁻ charges, e.g., H-Cl, O-H); ΔEN ≥ 1.7 = ionic (electron transfer, e.g., Na-Cl, K-F). Electronegativity increases across periods (higher Z_eff) and decreases down groups (larger radius); F is most electronegative (3.98), Fr is least (~0.7). Polar bonds have dipole moments (μ = Q × r) pointing from δ⁺ to δ⁻. Percent ionic character = (1 - e^(-0.25(ΔEN)²)) × 100%; no bond is 100% ionic (Na-Cl ~70% ionic). Bond polarity affects molecular properties: solubility (polar dissolves in polar, nonpolar in nonpolar), boiling point (polar molecules have higher bp due to stronger intermolecular forces), reactivity (polar bonds more reactive), and spectroscopy (polar bonds absorb IR). Molecular polarity depends on both bond polarity AND geometry (CO₂ has polar bonds but nonpolar molecule due to symmetry). Applications include: pharmaceutical design (balance polar/nonpolar for bioavailability), surfactants (amphiphilic molecules with polar heads and nonpolar tails), solvent selection (match polarity to solute), and material design (hydrophilic vs. hydrophobic surfaces). Understanding bond polarity enables prediction of chemical behavior and rational molecular design across chemistry disciplines.
